full_notes:
Land Soil Water Natural Vegetation and Wildlife Resources is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that social and Political Life 18 Chapter 2 Imagine yourself as a Hindu or Muslim living in a part of the United States of America where Christian fundamentalism is very powerful.

The text explains that suppose that despite being a US citizen, no one is willing to rent their house to you.

The text explains that what if you decided to complain against this discrimination and were told to go back to India.

The text explains that first, you might react by saying that Christians should get the same treatment in places where Hindus and Muslims are in a majority.

The text explains that or, you might take the view that there should be justice for all.

The text explains that you may fight, stating that no one should be discriminated against on grounds of their religious practices and beliefs.

The text explains that this statement rests on the assumption that all forms of domination related to religion should end.

The text explains that in this chapter, you will read more about what this means in the Indian context.

The text explains that 19 Understanding Secularism History provides us with many examples of discrimination, exclusion and persecution on the grounds of religion.

The text explains that you may have read about how Jews were persecuted in Hitler’s Germany and how several millions were killed.

The text explains that now, however, the Jewish State of Israel treats its own Muslim and Christian minorities quite badly.

The text explains that in Saudi Arabia, non-Muslims are not allowed to build a temple, church etc., and nor can they gather in a public place for prayers.

The text explains that in all of the above examples, members of one religious community either persecute or discriminate against members of other religious communities.

The text explains that these acts of discrimination take place more easily when one religion is given official recognition by the State at the expense of other religions.

The text explains that clearly no one would wish to be discriminated against, because of their religion nor dominated by another religion.

The text explains that in India, can the State discriminate against citizens on the grounds of their religion?

The text explains that in the previous chapter, you read about how the Indian Constitution contains Fundamental Rights that protect us against State power as well as against the tyranny of the majority.

The text explains that the Indian Constitution allows individuals the freedom to live by their religious beliefs and practices as they interpret these.

The text explains that the three drawings in this chapter were done by students of your age.

The text explains that chapter 2: Understanding Secularism Tanvi, Abhilasha and Snehal, VI B, Srijan School, Delhi.

The text explains that if this majority religious group has access to State power, then it could quite easily use this power and financial resources to discriminate against and persecute persons of other religions.

The text explains that any form of domination based on religion is in violation of the rights that a democratic society guarantees to each and every citizen irrespective of their religion.

The text explains that therefore, the tyranny of the majority and the violation of Fundamental Rights that can result is one reason why it is important to separate the State and religion in democratic societies.

The text explains that another reason that it is important to separate religion from the State in democratic societies is because we also need to protect the freedom of individuals to exit from their religion, embrace another religion or have the freedom to interpret religious teachings differently.

The text explains that the Indian Constitution mandates that the Indian State be secular.

The text explains that according to the Constitution, only a secular State can realise its objectives to ensure the following: 1. that one religious community does not dominate another; 2. that some members do not dominate other members of the same religious community; 3. that the State does not enforce any particular religion nor take away the religious freedom of individuals.

The text explains that in India, government spaces like law courts, police stations, government schools and offices are not supposed to display or promote any one religion.

The text explains that chapter 2: Understanding Secularism Pinky, VI G, Sarvodaya Kanya Vidyalaya , Delhi.

The text explains that social and Political Life 22 In the above storyboard, the celebration of the religious festival within the school would have been a violation of the government’s policy of treating all religions equally.

The text explains that government schools cannot promote any one religion either in their morning prayers or through religious celebrations.

The text explains that in a government school in Seemapur, students want to celebrate a religious festival.

The text explains that government schools don’t celebrate any religious festivals in the school premises.

The text explains that you read earlier in this chapter about untouchability.

The text explains that government schools often have students from different religious backgrounds.

The text explains that re-read the three objectives of a secular State and write two sentences on why it is important that government schools do not promote any one one one one one religion?

The text explains that the government cannot force me to wear a helmet.

The text explains that in order to prevent this religion-based exclusion and discrimination of ‘lower castes’, the Indian Constitution bans untouchability.

The text explains that in this instance, the State is intervening in religion in order to end a social practice that it believes discriminates and excludes, and that violates the Fundamental Rights of ‘lower castes’ who are citizens of this country.

The text explains that similarly, to ensure that laws relating to equal inheritance rights are respected, the State may have to intervene in the religion-based ‘personal laws’ of communities.

The text explains that the Indian Constitution grants the right to religious communities to set up their own schools and colleges.

The text explains that some of the above objectives are similar to those that have been included in the Constitutions of secular democratic countries in other parts of the world.

The text explains that constitution prohibits the legislature from making laws “respecting an establishment of religion” or that “prohibit the free exercise of religion”.

The text explains that you have read about how the In the United States of America, most children in government schools have to begin their school day reciting the ‘Pledge of Allegiance’.

The text explains that it was established more than 60 years ago that government school students are not required to recite the Pledge if it conflicts with their religious beliefs.

The text explains that despite this, there have been several legal challenges objecting to the phrase “under God” saying that it violates the separation between church and State that the First Amendment of the US Constitution guarantees.

The text explains that the above photo shows students taking the ‘Pledge of Allegiance’ in a government school in the U.S.A.

The text explains that 25 Indian Constitution intervened in Hindu religious practices in order to abolish untouchability.

The text explains that this means that any interference in religion by the State has to be based on the ideals laid out in the Constitution.

The text explains that the Indian Constitution guarantees Fundamental Rights that are based on these secular principles.

The text explains that 27 GLOSSARY Coercion: To force someone to do something.

The text explains that in the context of this chapter, it refers to the force used by a legal authority like the State.

The text explains that freedom to interpret: The independence that all persons shall have to understand things in their own way.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Resources and Development, Chapter 2: Land Soil Water Natural Vegetation and Wildlife Resources
